behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at handler/auth/queue boundary (reject + no enqueue), not just hash math. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: real OS/network not required; in-process HTTP app wiring is sufficient. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: if signature verification were a pure function tested alone, Unit could be enough. TOP2-CONTRAST (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because we must prove 'not forwarded' across module handoff; would flip to Unit if behavior were only 'verify HMAC function returns false for mismatch.' REJECTION-GATE: rejected System because behavior text only requires HTTP status and forwarding decision, not real network/subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start the app in-process with real middleware chain (including raw-body capture) and a test queue adapter. Send a request with intentionally incorrect X-Signature-256 over the same handler entrypoint used in production. Assert 401 and assert queue write method was not invoked.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: ordering bug appears at handler-to-queue persistence boundary. REAL-INFRA: no mandatory external OS/network primitive beyond local module wiring; local persistent store is enough. MOCK-HIDE: a naive mock that resolves immediately can hide ordering mistakes, so use real queue implementation against local persistence. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: if persistence layer timing differs in production infra, System could be needed. TOP2-CONTRAST (Integration vs System): Integration wins because required failure is ordering in local components; would flip to System if queue persistence depended on external broker ACK timing.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with real queue implementation backed by test local persistence. Instrument response completion timestamp and durable write completion point. Assert durable queue write completes before 200 response is emitted.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is orchestration between request path and async downstream trigger. REAL-INFRA: no real network/subprocess required; in-process wiring can expose accidental await/block. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: if only one function call chain is involved, Unit may suffice. TOP2-CONTRAST (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because we must observe response lifecycle while downstream module is intentionally slow; would flip to Unit if behavior only checked a single function returns before awaiting a local promise.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process HTTP invocation with downstream processor stubbed to a controllable long-running promise. Assert response is sent immediately (before resolving that promise) and downstream completion can occur later.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: explicit wall-clock SLA under concurrency depends on runtime scheduler, socket handling, and real timing behavior. MOCK-HIDE: fake timers/in-process stubs can miss event-loop starvation and queue contention. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: if implementation is fully deterministic and load-free, Integration might catch it. TOP2-CONTRAST (System vs Integration): System wins because the behavior names wall-clock latency under concurrent slow work; would flip to Integration if requirement were only logical non-blocking (no timed SLA). REJECTION-GATE: rejected Integration because text explicitly requires 'within 3 seconds wall-clock time' under concurrency.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real Node HTTP server process, generate concurrent traffic where earlier events trigger slow downstream work, then measure end-to-end request latency from client side. Assert acknowledgments for new valid requests remain <3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: duplicate suppression is a state handoff issue between handler and persistent queue/idempotency store. REAL-INFRA: local persistent DB/filesystem is enough; no real external network required. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: if dedupe is pure in-memory set logic, Unit may be enough. TOP2-CONTRAST (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because persistence/state across requests is core to failure mode; would flip to Unit if behavior text constrained dedupe to a pure function with no storage boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke handler twice with same event ID using real queue persistence layer in test mode. Assert first request enqueues once and second request does not create an additional queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: behavior is mapping a queue-layer condition to HTTP contract across module boundary. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess needed; queue-full signal can be produced by wired local modules. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: could be Unit if handler branch is trivial and queue signal is just an enum. TOP2-CONTRAST (Integration vs Unit): Integration wins because queue error translation is a cross-module contract; would flip to Unit if no queue module interaction existed and only static response formatting were tested.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with queue adapter that can emit a concrete 'full' condition (prefer real adapter error type). Send valid event and assert HTTP 503 plus correct Retry-After header is returned.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this verifies queue implementation plus persistence/state accounting, not just API shape. REAL-INFRA: local DB/filesystem-backed queue fits Integration; System only if external broker/OS-specific behavior is required. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. ADVERSARIAL-CRITIQUE: if capacity enforcement depends on broker-specific runtime semantics, System could be needed. TOP2-CONTRAST (Integration vs System): Integration wins because behavior text says 'persistent queue' without requiring external process/network; would flip to System if queue were a real external service (e.g., Redis/Kafka) whose true rejection semantics must be exercised. REJECTION-GATE: rejected System due no concrete text requiring external broker/network/subprocess.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate real persistent queue in test configuration with small capacity (e.g., 2). Enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert explicit rejection/error indicating capacity exceeded.
